title: Novel cellulose derivatives. IV. Preparation and thermal analysis of waxy esters of cellulose
abstract:
Novel Cellulose Derivatives. IV. Preparation and Thermal 
Analysis of Waxy Esters of Cellulose 
JAMES E. SEALEY, GAMIN1 SAMARANAYAKE, JASON G. TODD, and WOLFGANG G. GLASSER* 
Department of Wood Science and Forest Products and Biobased Materials Center, Virginia Tech, Blacksburg, 
Virginia 24061 -0324 
SYNOPSIS 
Cellulose esters with linear aliphatic acyl substituents ranging in size from CI2 (lauric acid) 
to C,, (eicosanoic acid) were prepared in homogeneous solution (DMAc/LiCl) using a novel 
synthetic method based on the use of a mixedp-toluenesulfonic/carboxylic acid anhydride. 
The resulting waxy cellulose esters had a high degree of substitution (DS), between 2.8 
and 2.9, and showed little degradation. Thermal analysis of these cellulose derivatives by 
differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and dynamic mechanical thermal analysis (DMTA) 
revealed a series of transitions that represented motion by both ester substituents and 
cellulosic main chain. Broad crystallization and melting transitions attributed to side-chain 
crystallinity were observed in the range between -19 and +55”C; these side-chain T, and 
T, transition temperatures increased by 10°C per carbon atom of the ester substituent. 
The Tg 
of these derivatives increased linearly with increasing substituent size from 94°C 
for Clz (cellulose laurate) to 134OC for Cz0 (cellulose eicosanoate). Evidence of “main- 
chain” crystallization was not observed for these samples, except in the case of peracetylated 
C,, and C,, esters, which had T,,, values of 96OC and 107”C, respectively. 0 
1996 John Wiley 
& Sons, Inc. 
Keywords: cellulose ester cellulose esterification cellulose acylation thermal analysis 
melting point glass transition side-chain crystallinity 
INTRODUCTION 
Cellulose esters represent a class of commercially 
important, thermoplastic polymers with excellent 
fiber and film-forming characteristics. Long-chain 
aliphatic acid esters of cellulose (“fatty acid esters” 
or “waxy esters”) have been identified as potential 
biodegradable plastics due to the enzymatically labile 
ester bond and the natural abundance of both cel- 
lulose and fatty acids.’ Similar attention has been 
focused on fatty acid esters of starch.2 Cellulose es- 
ters are produced commercially by heterogeneous- 
phase reaction chemistry using acid anhydrides and 
sulfuric acid catalyst, but this process is limited to 
short-chain esters of length of four carbons (C4, 
bu- 
tyryl) or less.3 Cellulose esters of higher aliphatic 
acids have been prepared by heterogeneous reactions

Journal of Polymer Science: Part B: Polymer Physics, Vol. 34,1613-1620 (1996)

using acid chlorides and ~ y r i d i n e , ~ , ~  
acid chlorides 
under vacuum,’ and aliphatic acids with trifluo- 
roacetic acid.6 
Reaction rates and final degree of substitution 
(DS) in heterogeneous reactions are hindered by low 
accessibility of solid cellulose to the esterification 
reagents.’ Several novel cellulose solvent systems 
employing combinations of organic and inorganic 
reagents have been reported within the past 25 
Accessibility of cellulose in solution is 
greatly improved, allowing all of the cellulose to be 
exposed to reactants simultaneously. Reactions in 
solution are able to proceed under milder conditions 
than analogous heterogeneous reactions, resulting 
in less degradation of cellulose. The resulting sub- 
stituents (in homogeneous-phase reactions) may 
cover all or only a portion of the available hydroxyl 
groups, with substituents uniformly distributed 
along the cellulose backbone. 
In a homologous series of near-fully substituted 
n-alkyl esters of cellulose (prepared under hetero- 
1613 
 1614 
SEALEY ET AL. 
geneous conditions), the apparent melting point (by 
visual observation and thermomechanical analysis) 
declined sharply with increasing substituent length 
(Fig. 1) from about 300°C for C2 (acetate) to ap- 
proximately 100°C for c6 (hexanoate). The apparent 
T, increased slightly from C8 (octanoate) to c18 
  teara ate).^.^ Dielectric thermal analysis (DETA) 
revealed Tg values of approximately 220"C, 9O"C, 
and 95°C for C2, C5, and C16 esters, respectively.6 
Additionally, a previous studyg reported, from DSC, 
a Te of 74°C and a T, of 115°C for fully substituted 
cellulose hexanoate (c6). Therefore, the Tg parallels 
the behavior of T,, decreasing with increasing side- 
chain length from C2 to c6, but converging with the 
apparent T,,, 
as the chain length increases beyond 
c6 (Fig. 1). For cellulose esters of length C6 and be- 
low, T, and Te are clearly identified as separate 
transitions. Cellulose esters with longer aliphatic 
(fatty or waxy esters) have less well-defined thermal 
transitions, and this is addressed in the present 
study. 
The previous reportg of this publication series 
dealt with thermal transitions of cellulose mixed es- 
ters having butyryl and hexanoyl functionality in 
addition to acetyl groups. It was of interest in this 
study to explore the nature of the thermal transi- 
tions of cellulose esters having alkyl substituents in 
the range of C12 to C2,,. Another objective of this 
study was to test the reactivity of a novel acylation 
system3 employing carboxylic acids, p-toluenesul- 
fonyl chloride (TsCl), and pyridine, with cellulose 
in homogeneous phase, dissolved in N, N-dimeth- 
ylacetamide/lithium chloride (DMAc/LiCl). Fully 
substituted esters of bulky "waxy" acids were to be 
synthesized using the novel reaction method and 
the resulting cellulose derivatives were to be ex- 
amined in terms of their thermal characteristics in 
relation to acyl substituent size. 
EXPERIMENTAL 
Materials 
Whatman CF-11 microcrystalline cellulose (What- 
man Chemicals, Hillsboro, OR) was used as the 
starting material in all reactions. Cellulose solutions 
of approximately 4 wt % were prepared in DMAc/ 
LiCl (9 wt %) as previously described.''p-Toluene- 
sulfonyl chloride (TsC1); lauric (CI2), myristic (C14), 
palmitic (CIS), and eicosanoic (C2,,) acids; solvents; 
and other reagents were obtained from Aldrich 
Chemical Company (Milwaukee, WI) and used as 
received.

Figure 1. Melting points and glass transitions of cel- 
lulose triesters as a function of acyl substituent size. (0) 
Apparent T, (visual observation, data from Malm et aL3); 
(0) apparent T,,, (visual observation and TMA, Morooka 
et al?); (A) Tg (DETA, Morooka et a1.6). 
Esterification Reaction 
Pyridine (3 eq/TsCl) was added dropwise to 25 mL 
of a 4% cellulose solution in DMAc/LiCl, magnet- 
ically stirred, at 25"C, under a dry nitrogen atmo- 
sphere. A solution in DMAc of alkanoic acid (2 eq/ 
cellulose OH) was added dropwise to the cellulose 
solution, followed by a solution (in DMAc) of TsCl 
(2 eq/OH). The reaction temperature was then in- 
creased to 50°C and held there for 24 h, except in 
the case of the esterification with eicosanoic acid 
(C2,,), which was heated to 65°C to facilitate disso- 
lution of the C2' acid. The products were isolated as 
white powders by precipitation into 50/50 methanol/ 
H20, purified by soxhlet extraction with methanol 
for 24 h, and vacuum dried at 55°C for 18 h. 
Characterization of Products 
Degree of substitution (DS) of the cellulose esters 
was determined by integration of 'H NMR spectra," 
recorded at ambient temperature in CDC13/2% tri- 
fluoroacetic acid with TMS as the internal standard, 
on a Varian 400 MHz NMR spectrometer. All esters 
had similar 'H NMR spectra. Typically, the follow- 
ing signals were recorded (all 6-scale in ppm): al- 
kanoyl chain, 0.9 (a group of triplets, CH3), 1.3 
(multiplet, -CH,-), 1.5 (multiplet, P-CH,), 2.0-2.5 
(multiplet, a,,,-CH2); cellulose main chain, 3.3-5.3 
(multiplet 1,2,3,4,5, and 6 ring protons). DS values 
from NMR were confirmed by gas chromatography 
(GC); a Varian 3700 gas chromatograph with a 
Hewlett Packard Model 3394A integrator and an 
OV-17 packed-glass column was used for GC anal-

JAMES E. SEALEY, GAMIN1 SAMARANAYAKE, JASON G. TODD, and WOLFGANG G. GLASSER*

Cellulose esters with linear aliphatic acyl substituents ranging in size from CI2 (lauric acid)
to C,, (eicosanoic acid) were prepared in homogeneous solution (DMAc/LiCl) using a novel
synthetic method based on the use of a mixedp-toluenesulfonic/carboxylic acid anhydride.
The resulting waxy cellulose esters had a high degree of substitution (DS), between 2.8
and 2.9, and showed little degradation. Thermal analysis of these cellulose derivatives by
differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and dynamic mechanical thermal analysis (DMTA)
revealed a series of transitions that represented motion by both ester substituents and
cellulosic main chain. Broad crystallization and melting transitions attributed to side-chain
crystallinity were observed in the range between -19 and +55”C; these side-chain T, and
T, transition temperatures increased by 10°C per carbon atom of the ester substituent.
of these derivatives increased linearly with increasing substituent size from 94°C
for Clz (cellulose laurate) to 134OC for Cz0 (cellulose eicosanoate). Evidence of “main-
chain” crystallization was not observed for these samples, except in the case of peracetylated
C,, and C,, esters, which had T,,, values of 96OC and 107”C, respectively. 0

dealt with thermal transitions of cellulose mixed es-
ters having butyryl and hexanoyl functionality in
addition to acetyl groups. It was of interest in this
study to explore the nature of the thermal transi-
tions of cellulose esters having alkyl substituents in
the range of C12 to C2,,. Another objective of this

fonyl chloride (TsCl), and pyridine, with cellulose
in homogeneous phase, dissolved in N, N-dimeth-
ylacetamide/lithium chloride (DMAc/LiCl). Fully

the resulting cellulose derivatives were to be ex-

sphere. A solution in DMAc of alkanoic acid (2 eq/

creased to 50°C and held there for 24 h, except in

Degree of substitution (DS) of the cellulose esters
was determined by integration of 'H NMR spectra,"
recorded at ambient temperature in CDC13/2% tri-
fluoroacetic acid with TMS as the internal standard,
on a Varian 400 MHz NMR spectrometer. All esters
had similar 'H NMR spectra. Typically, the follow-
ing signals were recorded (all 6-scale in ppm): al-
kanoyl chain, 0.9 (a group of triplets, CH3), 1.3
(multiplet, -CH,-), 1.5 (multiplet, P-CH,), 2.0-2.5
(multiplet, a,,,-CH2); cellulose main chain, 3.3-5.3
(multiplet 1,2,3,4,5, and 6 ring protons). DS values
from NMR were confirmed by gas chromatography
(GC); a Varian 3700 gas chromatograph with a
Hewlett Packard Model 3394A integrator and an
OV-17 packed-glass column was used for GC anal-
ysis in accordance with previous work." Molecular
weights of the materials were determined by gel per-
meation chromatography (GPC) with universal cal-
ibration as described elsewhere."."
Thermal decomposition temperatures of the cellu-
lose esters were determined by thermogravimetric
analysis (TGA) on a Perkin-Elmer TGS-2. Samples
were heated at 5"C/min under dry nitrogen atmo-
sphere. The thermal decomposition temperature
(TJ was reported as the onset of significant (>0.5%)
weight loss from the heated sample.
Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was
performed on a Perkin-Elmer DSC-4. Cellulose es-
ter samples, in powder form as obtained from the
reaction as described above, were placed in alumi-
num DSC pans. Sample weight was between 5 and
10 mg. Samples were heated from -50°C to 180°C
at 20"C/min for a total of three scans. The cooling
rate was ZO"C/min between first and second scans,
and ca. 50"C/min between subsequent heating scans
(DSC heating curves of these samples were identical
regardless of the cooling rate.) Melting point (T,)
values were reported as the peak temperatures of
melt endotherms recorded on the third heating scan.
Glass transition temperature (T,)
corded as the midpoint of the heat capacity change
in the glass transition region during the third heating
Dynamic mechanical thermal analysis (DMTA)
experiments were carried out with a Polymer Lab-
oratories Mk I DMTA with a standard bending head.
Samples were cast into films from 5% solution in
chloroform at room temperature. Films were heated
between Teflon sheets at 110°C for 1.5 h in the vac-
uum oven in order to remove residual solvent. Disks,
8 mm dia. X 0.2 mm thickness, were cut from the
films and clamped in shear mode. Samples were
tested at frequencies from 0.1 to 30 Hz, over a tem-
perature range from -100°C to 2OO0C, at a heating
anhydrides. Derivatives with DS between 2.8 and
2.9 (out of a possible maximum DS of 3.0 hydroxyls
substituted per cellulose repeat unit) for C12, C14,
C18, and Cz0 acids were obtained using a stoichio-
metric ratio of 2 equivalents of acid per cellulose
hydroxyl. Elemental analysis of these derivatives
established that no significant tosylation or chlori-
nation had taken place. Molecular weight data (by
GPC, Table I) suggested that no significant degra-
dation had occurred except for the Czo derivative,
which was heated to 65°C (instead of the normal
50°C) to dissolve the eicosanoic acid. The average
degrees of polymerization (DP) in Table I for C12,
C14, and C18 compare well with accepted values, DP,
172 and DP, 300, for the CF-11 starting material
as determined in our laboratory (by GPC of tricarb-
anilated CF-11). Severe degradation of cellulose
was observed when the reaction temperature ex-
Thermal decomposition temperatures ( Td) ranged
from 208°C for cellulose laurate to 218°C for cel-
lulose eicosanoate (Table I). Significant mass loss
with time under isothermal heating at 200°C was
observed for cellulose myristate; therefore, DSC
scans were limited to a maximum temperature of
180°C to avoid sample decomposition. As an addi-
tional precaution, the DSC samples were held at
180°C for less than 2 min between each scan.
Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)
Thermal analysis by DSC revealed two separate and
distinct transitions for the waxy cellulose esters: a
broad T, occurring in the neighborhood of lOO"C,
and a broad, low-temperature melt endotherm (peak
(Fig. 2). The second-order transition at around
100°C that was designated as Tg remained subtle
during both heating and cooling. Annealing (at
110°C for 1 h) sharpened this transition and some-
times induced a small enthalpy relaxation peak (not

The Tm,L endotherm was attributed to melting of
side-chain crystals. Analysis of C,, through C18 esters
by DETA in a separate study6 revealed a transition
that was assigned to the freezing point of micro-
Brownian motion of the ester side chain; the tem-
peratures reported for this transition, -15°C for C,,
increasing to 34°C for C18, are in excellent agreement
with the T,,L values obtained in the present study.
Side-chain crystallization has been reported in other
polymers with stiff backbones and long n-alkyl side
chains, such as poly(n-alkyl thiophenes)" and

Long-term annealing of the cellulose eicosanoate
(Czo) sample had the effect of splitting the side-chain

other, broader peak occurring at 50°C corresponds

responding to the glass transition and side-chain
melting processes, respectively (Fig. 6). Data for the
cellulose myristate (C14) sample were omitted be-
cause the thickness of the film (0.1 mm) cast from
this sample was not adequate for analysis. For the
cellulose laurate sample, there was a slight drop in
the shear storage modulus, G, accompanying side-
chain melting in the vicinity of -2OoC, whereas a
pronounced drop in G and associated loss tangent
(tan 6) peak occurred in the glass transition region
(ca. 100OC). The modulus drop accompanying side-
chain melting was much more pronounced for the
CIS and especially the C2, esters, and a well-defined
loss tangent peak occurred in the CZ0 sample at 7OoC
(Fig. 7). This peak did not shift with frequency in
the range tested from 0.1 to 30 Hz. The samples
retained substantial modulus up to the glass tran-
sition. Values taken from peaks of the loss modulus
curve (not shown) for the a and p transitions (Table
111) agreed approximately but not precisely with the
Tg and Tm,L transitions from DSC.
Explanation of Cellulose Ester Thermal Transition
The melting and glass transition behavior of cellu-
lose aliphatic triesters can be explained as follows.
As the ester substituent length increases from C2 to
c6, the Tg decreases due to increased spacing be-
tween cellulose chains and dilution of secondary
bonding, increasing the mobility of the cellulose
chains (i.e., a plasticization effect). Similarly, the
T, declines as the substituent length increases owing
to increasing entropy of the melt (resulting in in-
creased AS,) and decreasing enthalpy of melting
AHm (at equilibrium, T,,, = AHJAS,). However, as
the ester length becomes larger than C6, Tg levels
off and begins to increase slightly as steric hindrance
of neighboring bulky substituents restricts rotation
about the glycosidic ether linkages of the cellulose
backbone, stiffening the chain. The gap between T,
and Tg therefore narrows, as it indeed does? as side-
chain length reaches c6 to c8. Beyond c6 to cs, cel-
lulose esters fail to exhibit melt transitions except
in the case of peracetylated CI2 and C14 esters as
described in the following paragraph.
Peracetylated Cellulose Waxy Esters
The cellulose esters prepared for this study all had
a DS of 2.8 or 2.9; that is, a few hydroxyls remained
unsubstituted. A few free hydroxyls can have a sig-
nificant effect on thermal properties through hy-
drogen bonding. Therefore, the waxy ester samples
were treated with acetic anhydride/pyridine to sub-
stitute the remaining free hydroxyls with acetyl
groups. The resulting derivatives showed (by DSC)
side-chain melting peaks in the same locations as
the unacetylated samples (Table IV). Curiously, a
second endotherm (designated T m , H )  was seen in the
Dynamic Mechanical Relaxations" of
By DMTA; peaks in shear loss modulus at 1 hz.
Thermal Transitions of Peracetylated
C12 and C14 peracetylated samples at 96°C and
107"C, respectively; this was in the former location
of the Tg in the unacetylated samples. This endo-
therm seems too large and broad to be attributed to
enthalpy relaxation associated with the Tg; instead,
it is likely due to the melting a of main-chain crystal
structure (although it is difficult to be sure of this
without X-ray diffraction experiments). A corre-
sponding crystallization exotherm was seen in the
DSC cooling scan (Fig. 8). The peracetylated C18
and Cz0 esters did not show this high-temperature
melt endotherm, but instead displayed glass tran-
sitions as seen before, but at temperatures 25-30°C
lower than the original unacetylated C18 and Cz0 es-
ter glass transitions (Table IV). Apparently, acet-
ylation of the few remaining hydroxyls reduced the
Tg of the cellulose esters sufficiently to allow crys-
tallization to occur in the C12 and C14 samples but
not in the C18 and Cz0 samples. (Recall that the main-
chain T, declines with increasing side-chain size.)
Annealing the cellulose myristate (C14) sample
for 1 h at 60°C shifted the Tm,H endotherm to a
higher temperature range and revealed what ap-
peared to be a glass transition at 65"C, 30" higher
than the Tg of the unacetylated sample (Fig. 8, curve
C). Annealing the same sample for 1 h at 80°C re-
vealed a sharp peak at 90°C in addition to the usual
broad endotherm; this 90°C peak may be due to en-
thalpy relaxation associated with the Tr Unknown
remains the possible effect of a hypothetical liquid
crystalline order, an order of which cellulose deriv-
atives are doubtlessly capable.16.17
Reaction of cellulose in DMAC/LiCl solution was
found (again) to be highly effective, avoiding the
problems of low reagent accessibility of solid cellu-
lose in heterogeneous reactions. Esterification of
cellulose via the carboxylic acid/toluenesulfonyl
chloride/pyridine method was discovered to be an
efficient method for modification, resulting in de-
rivatives with high DS and a low degree of cellulose
Side-chain crystallization occurred in linear ali-
phatic esters of cellulose when the ester substituent
is 12 or more carbon atoms in length. The melting
points and heats of fusion of side-chain crystals in-
creased with increasing ester length, due to increas-
ing crystal thickness, as more methylene units are
able to participate in crystallization at longer side-
chain lengths. Side-chain melting points increased
from -15°C for cellulose laurate ( C12) to 55°C for
cellulose eicosanoate ( Cz0) .
It is evident from the results of this study that
the slight increase noted in the apparent T, of cel-
lulose triesters (from visual observation and sample
flow, Fig. 1) as ester substituent length increased
beyond CI2 is actually an increase in the Tg with
increasing substituent size. This is to say that the
apparent T, from visual observation is actually due
to softening above Tg as substituent size exceeds
about C8. The Tg (midpoint value) increased from
94°C for DS 2.9 cellulose laurate ( C12) to 134°C for
DS 2.9 cellulose eicosanoate ( Cz0) . Previous studies
had shown that the (main chain) T, and Tg of tri-
substituted cellulose esters decline with increasing
substituent size from C2 to C6, and converge as ester
size reaches C8. For CI2 through Cz0 esters with DS
of 2.8 to 2.9, main-chain crystallization was not ob-
served. The Tg was lowered by ca. 30°C when the
few remaining hydroxyls were acetylated, allowing
the C12 and C14 cellulose esters, but not the C18 and

tate (C14). (A) Heating scan after cooling at ca. 50°C/min
from 15OoC, (B) cooling scan at 20°C/min, (C) heating
